sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Jerry, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his son.

He reported that his son, who is 10 years old, is more aggressive in playing with him than with his peers, and during a game, Jerry landed awkwardly and twisted his ankle, resulting in bruising and swelling on the outside of his ankle.
He has been experiencing difficulty walking and instability in his ankle, which is a concern as he has recently resumed a regular workout routine and does not want to disrupt his progress.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The ankle was swollen, with exquisite tenderness to palpation on the lateral side and some tenderness on the medial deltoid region.
The skin is intact, and capillary refill is brisk.
There is no malalignment.
Test Results:
X-ray of the right ankle: A displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Jerry has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture of the right ankle, which is likely to require surgery to stabilize the ankle and promote healing.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; right ankle instability.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for pain management and inflammation.
I advised Jerry to ice his ankle and keep it elevated, especially at work, since he works from home.
I recommended using crutches to avoid putting weight on the ankle and to prevent further injury.
I also suggested that he continue coaching his son's basketball team, as this will help him stay active and engaged.
Follow-up: I plan to see Jerry in three months to assess the healing of his fracture and to begin rehabilitation exercises to restore his ankle stability.
